content.text: Refactoring AI integration code carries a risk that does not exist in traditional refactors: the system's outputs are non-deterministic, so standard unit test suites cannot confirm behavioral equivalence. Eval regression suites fill this gap by capturing the behavioral surface of an agentic system before a refactor and asserting that it is preserved after the refactor.
What it is

A refactor safety eval regression suite is a collection of automated behavioral tests that run against an agentic AI system on every pull request that changes integration code. Unlike unit tests that assert functional correctness of deterministic code, eval regression tests assert that the distribution of outputs produced by the system on a fixed set of inputs meets quality thresholds — thresholds established against the pre-refactor baseline.

The suite has four layers: a golden set of fixed input-output pairs that must match (or nearly match) after refactoring; a semantic similarity check that asserts outputs remain close in meaning even when phrasing changes; an LLM-as-judge scoring layer that rates each output on a quality rubric and fails the build if the average score drops below the baseline; and a latency and cost budget check that fails the build if the refactor has degraded performance or increased token consumption.

Critically, the suite is designed as a CI gate: every refactor PR must pass all four layers before merge is permitted. The suite does not merely report regressions; it blocks the refactor from reaching the main branch until the regression is resolved or the threshold is explicitly updated with PM and engineering lead approval.
Why it matters

A prompt extraction refactor that moves a system prompt from an inline f-string to a Jinja2 template may preserve the template's content exactly but subtly change whitespace, capitalization, or escape behavior in ways that meaningfully alter model outputs. A retry logic refactor that adds exponential backoff may change the order in which tokens are generated during streaming, breaking downstream parsers that expect a specific response structure. These are real regressions caused by refactors that look correct on code review.

The challenge is that correctness for AI system output is not binary. A refactored system that produces outputs that are 95 percent semantically equivalent to the original is probably acceptable; one that produces outputs that are 70 percent semantically equivalent may have broken something important. Humans reviewing PRs cannot make this judgment at code review time without running the eval suite. The eval regression suite makes this judgment automatically and surfaces it in the CI pipeline where it can be acted on before merge.

Beyond the immediate refactoring use case, building an eval regression suite as a CI gate creates a permanent safety net for the system. Every future prompt change, model upgrade, tool modification, or library update runs against the same suite. The investment in building it for refactoring safety returns dividends on every future change to the system.
Benefits
Automated behavioral equivalence check: the eval suite confirms that refactored code produces outputs within acceptable thresholds without requiring manual testing of every change.
Catches non-obvious regressions: whitespace changes in prompts, altered escaping, changed retry order, and modified serialization are all surfaces that unit tests miss but eval tests catch.
Creates a baseline record: each successful eval run creates a timestamped quality baseline, making it easy to track behavioral drift over time independent of refactoring.
Enables faster refactoring cycles: engineers can refactor aggressively knowing that the eval suite will catch regressions, rather than being conservative out of fear of undetected behavioral changes.
Forces eval investment: requiring eval suite passage as a merge gate forces the team to build and maintain a high-quality eval suite rather than leaving it as an optional nice-to-have.
Trade-offs
LLM API cost: running the eval suite on every PR requires LLM API calls for each test case; at 100 test cases per suite run, costs can reach 1 to 5 USD per PR depending on model and test complexity.
Non-determinism complicates thresholds: because LLM outputs vary across runs, thresholds must be set wide enough to accommodate natural variation without masking genuine regressions.
False negatives on edge cases: the golden set cannot cover every possible input; a refactor may regress behavior on inputs not in the suite while passing all suite tests.
LLM-as-judge reliability: the judge model used for quality scoring may itself drift over time if the judge model is updated, causing previously passing scores to fail or vice versa.
Build time increase: eval suite CI steps add 2 to 10 minutes to pipeline execution time, increasing feedback cycles for refactoring PRs.
How it works

The eval suite receives a fixed set of test cases, each consisting of an input (typically a user message or task description), a reference output (the expected correct output), and a set of evaluation criteria specific to that test case. The suite runs each input through the refactored system and collects the actual outputs.

For each test case, four checks run in parallel. The golden set check computes a similarity score between the actual output and the reference output using embedding cosine similarity or a configurable matching function. The semantic similarity check embeds both actual and reference outputs and asserts the cosine similarity exceeds the configured threshold (typically 0.88 to 0.92). The LLM-as-judge check prompts a judge model with the task, the reference output, and the actual output, asking it to score the actual output on a 1-to-5 rubric covering accuracy, completeness, and safety; scores below the baseline average fail the check. The cost guard measures the token count of the actual output and fails if it exceeds the reference token count by more than a configured margin.

The suite aggregates results across all test cases and produces a pass/fail verdict plus a diff report showing which test cases changed, in which direction, and by how much. The PR cannot merge until the verdict is PASS.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Eval regression suite: refactor PR triggers CI eval step with golden set, LLM-as-judge, latency budget, and cost guard checks; pass allows merge, fail blocks; post-merge monitoring watches production metrics

content.text: The diagram shows the full eval safety gate: the refactor PR triggers the eval suite CI step, which runs four checks in parallel. A PASS allows merge and hands off to the post-merge production monitor. A FAIL blocks merge and requires the author to fix the regression. The component breakdown at the bottom shows each check's purpose and failure criterion.
When to use it

Run the eval regression suite on every PR that modifies: system prompt files or prompt templates; the LLM gateway layer (prompt construction, response parsing); the model adapter (any code that calls the LLM API directly); the retry and resilience wrapper; output validation or schema enforcement code; or the serialization/deserialization of model inputs and outputs.

The suite is not required on PRs that touch only infrastructure, CI configuration, documentation, or tests themselves (unless those tests affect how the system calls the model). Apply good judgment: a PR that changes only a log message format does not need to pay the eval suite's API cost.

Build the eval suite before or concurrent with the first major refactoring sprint, not after. The suite must have a baseline to compare against; if it is built after the refactor, the refactored state becomes the baseline and any regressions introduced by the refactor are invisible.
How to implement
Define the test case schema: a test case has an input (string or dict), a reference output (string), an evaluation criteria set (list of rubric dimensions), and a cost budget (max token count).
Curate the golden set: collect 50 to 200 representative input-output pairs from production logs, capturing the diverse input types and edge cases the system must handle.

Implement the golden set check: compute embedding cosine similarity between actual and reference outputs using a text embedding model; set the failure threshold based on observed natural variation (run the same inputs through the unmodified system multiple times to calibrate).

Implement the LLM-as-judge check: design a scoring prompt that rates outputs on a 1-to-5 rubric covering the dimensions that matter for your system (accuracy, safety, format compliance); establish the baseline score distribution before the first refactor.
Implement the cost guard: extract token counts from the model response's usage metadata and compare against a per-test-case budget with a configurable slack percentage.
Wire the suite into CI as a required check on PRs that touch eval-relevant files; configure the job to fail the PR build on any check failure and to post the diff report as a PR comment.
Examples
Eval regression suite runner

# eval_regression_suite.py
# Runs a behavioral regression suite against the current system state.
# Intended as a CI step on refactoring PRs.
# Usage: python eval_regression_suite.py --subject agentic-ai

import json
import argparse
import sys
from pathlib import Path
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Callable
import anthropic
import numpy as np

@dataclass
class TestCase:
    id: str
    input: str
    reference_output: str
    rubric: list[str]          # dimensions for LLM-as-judge
    max_output_tokens: int     # cost budget

@dataclass
class TestResult:
    case_id: str
    actual_output: str
    similarity_score: float    # cosine similarity vs reference
    judge_score: float         # 1.0 to 5.0
    actual_tokens: int
    max_tokens: int
    passed: bool
    failure_reason: str = ""

SIMILARITY_THRESHOLD = 0.88
JUDGE_SCORE_THRESHOLD = 3.5   # out of 5
TOKEN_SLACK_PCT = 0.20         # allow 20% more tokens than reference

def embed(text: str, client: anthropic.Anthropic) -> list[float]:
    # Use any embedding model; here we illustrate with a placeholder.
    # In production, use OpenAI ada-002, Cohere embed, or voyage-2.
    # This function must remain stable between baseline and refactor runs.
    raise NotImplementedError("Replace with your embedding implementation")

def cosine_similarity(a: list[float], b: list[float]) -> float:
    a_arr, b_arr = np.array(a), np.array(b)
    return float(np.dot(a_arr, b_arr) / (np.linalg.norm(a_arr) * np.linalg.norm(b_arr)))

def llm_judge_score(
    input_text: str,
    reference: str,
    actual: str,
    rubric: list[str],
    client: anthropic.Anthropic,
) -> float:
    rubric_str = "\n".join(f"- {r}" for r in rubric)
    prompt = f"""You are an expert evaluator. Score the ACTUAL output on a 1-5 scale.

Task input: {input_text}
Reference output: {reference}
Actual output: {actual}

Rubric dimensions (score the worst dimension):
{rubric_str}

Respond with ONLY a number from 1 to 5.""".strip()
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5",
        max_tokens=10,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    )
    try:
        return float(resp.content[0].text.strip())
    except ValueError:
        return 1.0

def run_system(input_text: str, client: anthropic.Anthropic) -> tuple[str, int]:
    # Replace with your actual system under test.
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5",
        max_tokens=512,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": input_text}],
    )
    return resp.content[0].text, resp.usage.output_tokens

def evaluate(case: TestCase, client: anthropic.Anthropic) -> TestResult:
    actual, actual_tokens = run_system(case.input, client)

    ref_emb = embed(case.reference_output, client)
    act_emb = embed(actual, client)
    similarity = cosine_similarity(ref_emb, act_emb)

    judge = llm_judge_score(case.input, case.reference_output, actual, case.rubric, client)

    max_tokens = int(case.max_output_tokens * (1 + TOKEN_SLACK_PCT))
    passed = (
        similarity >= SIMILARITY_THRESHOLD
        and judge >= JUDGE_SCORE_THRESHOLD
        and actual_tokens <= max_tokens
    )
    failure_reason = ""
    if not passed:
        reasons = []
        if similarity < SIMILARITY_THRESHOLD:
            reasons.append(f"similarity {similarity:.3f} < {SIMILARITY_THRESHOLD}")
        if judge < JUDGE_SCORE_THRESHOLD:
            reasons.append(f"judge score {judge:.1f} < {JUDGE_SCORE_THRESHOLD}")
        if actual_tokens > max_tokens:
            reasons.append(f"tokens {actual_tokens} > budget {max_tokens}")
        failure_reason = "; ".join(reasons)

    return TestResult(
        case_id=case.id,
        actual_output=actual,
        similarity_score=similarity,
        judge_score=judge,
        actual_tokens=actual_tokens,
        max_tokens=max_tokens,
        passed=passed,
        failure_reason=failure_reason,
    )

def main():
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    parser.add_argument("--cases", default="evals/golden_set.json")
    args = parser.parse_args()

    cases = [TestCase(**c) for c in json.loads(Path(args.cases).read_text())]
    client = anthropic.Anthropic()

    results = [evaluate(c, client) for c in cases]
    failures = [r for r in results if not r.passed]

    print(f"Eval suite: {len(results)} cases, {len(failures)} failures")
    for f in failures:
        print(f"  FAIL [{f.case_id}]: {f.failure_reason}")

    if failures:
        sys.exit(1)
    print("All eval checks passed.")
    sys.exit(0)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
Golden set test case format

[
  {
    "id": "classify-001",
    "input": "I need to cancel my subscription immediately. I am very unhappy with the service.",
    "reference_output": "Category: Cancellation\nSentiment: Negative\nUrgency: High\nAction: Escalate to retention team",
    "rubric": [
      "Correctly identifies the ticket category",
      "Correctly identifies the sentiment polarity",
      "Correctly assesses urgency level",
      "Recommends an appropriate action"
    ],
    "max_output_tokens": 50
  },
  {
    "id": "summarize-001",
    "input": "Please summarize the following support thread in one sentence: [thread content here]",
    "reference_output": "Customer reported billing discrepancy resolved after account credit was applied.",
    "rubric": [
      "Summary is factually accurate relative to the thread",
      "Summary is concise (one sentence)",
      "No hallucinated details"
    ],
    "max_output_tokens": 40
  }
]
Real-world example

A team refactoring their customer intent classification agent from a monolithic LangChain chain to a layered gateway-adapter architecture ran the eval suite before and after the refactor. The suite had 120 golden-set test cases covering the six intent categories the agent classified.

The initial refactor introduced a subtle whitespace difference in the system prompt (a trailing newline was dropped) that shifted the agent's JSON output structure on 8 of 120 cases. The semantic similarity check caught 4 of these; the LLM-as-judge caught the remaining 4. The team identified the whitespace issue from the diff report within 15 minutes of the CI failure, corrected it, and re-ran the suite to confirm all 120 cases passed. Without the eval suite, this regression would have reached production and caused parsing failures in the downstream webhook consumer.
Common mistakes
Setting thresholds too tight

# WRONG: exact match threshold fails on natural variation
SIMILARITY_THRESHOLD = 0.999  # LLMs produce slightly different outputs each run

# CORRECT: calibrate threshold from natural variation baseline
# Run the same inputs through the unmodified system 5 times,
# compute pairwise similarities, use 5th percentile as threshold.
SIMILARITY_THRESHOLD = 0.88  # calibrated from natural variation

Thresholds set too tight produce false positives on every PR because natural LLM output variation will cause some cases to fail even when no behavioral regression has occurred. Calibrate thresholds from observed natural variation before the first refactoring sprint.
Not pinning the judge model version

If the LLM-as-judge model is updated (e.g., from claude-haiku-4-5 to a newer version), its scoring calibration may shift, causing previously passing scores to fail or vice versa. Pin the judge model version in the eval suite configuration and treat a judge model upgrade as a baseline reset requiring team review.
Best practices
Calibrate all thresholds before the first refactoring sprint by running the unmodified system five times on the golden set and computing the natural variation statistics.
Curate golden-set test cases from production logs, not from synthetic data; real user inputs expose the input distributions that matter.
Pin the judge model version and treat a judge upgrade as a baseline reset requiring team sign-off.
Cache embedding computations for reference outputs; they do not change between runs and are expensive to recompute.
Post the eval diff report as a PR comment automatically so the author sees which test cases changed and why without leaving the code review interface.
Maintain separate golden sets for each major input type or feature area so a regression in one area does not obscure passing results in others.
Performance and scalability

Eval suite CI cost is proportional to the number of test cases multiplied by the cost of the models used. At 100 test cases with claude-haiku-4-5 as the judge and as the system under test, a typical suite run costs between 0.50 and 2.00 USD and completes in 2 to 5 minutes with parallel execution. At 500 test cases the cost reaches 2.50 to 10.00 USD and completion time extends to 5 to 15 minutes.

Use parallelism aggressively: evaluate all test cases concurrently up to the API rate limit. Cache embedding vectors for reference outputs across runs. Consider running the full suite only on PRs that touch eval-relevant files, and a 20 percent random sample of the suite on all other PRs, to reduce cost on infrastructure and documentation PRs.
Tooling and ecosystem
LangSmith: a testing and evaluation platform from LangChain that provides golden set management, LLM-as-judge frameworks, and CI integration.
Braintrust: a purpose-built eval platform with baseline tracking, threshold management, and PR comment integration.
RAGAS: an evaluation framework specifically for RAG and agentic systems with built-in faithfulness, relevance, and correctness metrics.
pytest with vcr.py: for teams that prefer standard Python testing infrastructure, vcr.py records and replays LLM API responses, enabling deterministic eval reruns at zero cost.

Security and reliability

Test case inputs in the golden set may contain sensitive user data if sourced from production logs. Anonymize or synthesize all PII before adding cases to the golden set, which is stored in the repository and accessible to all contributors. Use a data anonymization pipeline when importing from production logs.

The eval suite CI job should have read-only access to the LLM API (inference only, no fine-tuning or model management permissions). Use a separate API key for the eval CI job with the minimum necessary permissions, and rotate it on the same schedule as other CI secrets. A compromised eval CI key can only read model outputs, not modify models or access training data.
Key takeaways
Eval regression suites are the safety net for refactoring non-deterministic AI systems — they confirm behavioral equivalence where unit tests cannot.
The four-layer suite (golden set similarity, semantic similarity, LLM-as-judge scoring, and cost guard) covers the failure modes that code review and unit tests miss.
Calibrate all thresholds from natural LLM output variation before the first refactoring sprint to avoid false positives.
Wire the suite as a required CI gate that blocks PR merge on failure; optional gates are ignored during time pressure.
Cache reference output embeddings, run test cases in parallel, and scope the suite to eval-relevant PRs to keep CI costs manageable.
Pin the judge model version and treat a judge upgrade as a baseline reset requiring explicit team review.
